    All Implemented Interfaces:
    113:
    NodeWithRange<Node>, NodeWithTokenRange<Node>, Observable, Visitable, HasParentNode<Node>, Cloneable
    114:

    Direct Known Subclasses:
    117:
    ArrayCreationLevel, BodyDeclaration, CatchClause, Comment, CompilationUnit, Expression, ImportDeclaration, MemberValuePair, Modifier, ModuleDeclaration, ModuleDirective, Name, PackageDeclaration, Parameter, Node 122: extends Object 123: implements Cloneable, HasParentNode<Node>, Visitable, NodeWithRange<Node>, NodeWithTokenRange<Node> 124:
    Base class for all nodes of the abstract syntax tree. 125:

    Construction

    126:

    The tree is built by instantiating the required nodes, then adding them to other nodes. 127: If it is the parser who is building the tree, it will use the largest constructor, 128: the one with "range" as the first parameter. 129: If you want to manually instantiate nodes, we suggest to... 130:

      131:
    • use a convenience method, like "addStatement(...)", or if none are available...
      • 132:
    • use a convenient constructor, like ClassOrInterfaceType(String name), or if none are available...
      • 133:
    • use the default constructor.
      • 134:
    • Alternatively, use one of the JavaParser.parse(snippet) methods.
      • 135:
    136: ... and use the various methods on the node to initialize it further, if needed. 137:

    Parent/child

    138:

    The parent node field is managed automatically and can be seen as read only. 139: Note that there is only one parent, 140: and trying to use the same node in two places will lead to unexpected behaviour. 141: It is advised to clone() a node before moving it around. 142:

    Comments

    143:

    Each Node can have one associated comment which describes it and 144: a number of "orphan comments" which it contains but are not specifically 145: associated to any child. 146:

    Positions

    147:

    When the parser creates nodes, it sets their source code position in the "range" field. 148: When you manually instantiate nodes, their range is not set. 149: The top left character is position 1, 1. 150: Note that since this is an abstract syntax tree, 151: it leaves out a lot of text from the original source file, 152: like where braces or comma's are exactly. 153: Therefore there is no position information on everything in the original source file. 154:

    Observers

    155:

    It is possible to add observers to the tree. 156: Any change in the tree is sent as an event to any observers watching. 157:

    Visitors

    158:

    The most comfortable way of working with an abstract syntax tree is using visitors. 159: You can use one of the visitors in the visitor package, or extend one of them. 160: A visitor can be "run" by calling accept on a node: 161: 

    node.accept(visitor, argument);
    162: where argument is an object of your choice (often simply null.)
    163:
